perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, the term "Web site" shall mean a computer system intended to be accessed through the World Wide Web segment of the Internet, including software and content intended to be viewed and/or operated upon by persons accessing the computer system via the Internet. A Web site may exist on a single computer system with other Web sites. The term "Hosted Site" shall mean the Web site of the Customer that is hosted by i-on under the terms and conditions of this Agreement. The term "Hosting Computer" shall mean the computer system and related equipment on which the Hosted Site exists.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, the term "Web site" shall mean a static collection of HTML pages and images. A Web site may exist on a single computer system with other Web sites. The term "Hosted Site" shall mean the Customer's digital presence hosted by i-on, inclusive of web applications, databases, and all related digital assets. The term "Hosting Computer" shall mean the virtual server environment on which the Hosted Site is deployed.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Web site' has been narrowed to 'a static collection of HTML pages and images,' while 'Hosted Site' now includes web applications and databases. This creates an in-text contradiction because the services provided (e.g., SQL Server database software) clearly indicate that the 'Web site' (as originally defined) is intended to include more than just static content. This contradiction affects the interpretation of i-on's responsibilities for maintaining the 'Hosted Site'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: use of the Microsoft SQL Server 6.5 or higher database server software for the Hosted Site, within the boundaries of allocated computer storage, per #5 above;
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'Web site' broadly, encompassing software and content. The changed text narrows the definition of 'Web site' to static HTML pages and images while simultaneously expanding the definition of 'Hosted Site' to include web applications and databases. This creates a contradiction because the 'Contradicted Text' refers to 'use of the Microsoft SQL Server 6.5 or higher database server software for the Hosted Site.' If a 'Web site' is now only static content, but the 'Hosted Site' (which contains the website) requires SQL Server database software, then the definition of 'Web site' contradicts the clause allowing for database functionality within the Hosted Site.

perturbation.original_text: Under this Agreement, i-on will provide the following limited services for the Hosted Site: 1. connectivity to the Internet via a T1 (that may be shared by other Web sites) to a leading Internet backbone access provider such as UUNET, and reasonable efforts to maintain such connectivity with the phone company and the Internet backbone access provider;
perturbation.changed_text: Under this Agreement, i-on will provide the following limited services for the Customer's Presence: 1. connectivity to the Internet via a shared connection to a leading Internet backbone access provider, and reasonable efforts to maintain such connectivity with the internet service provider;
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Hosted Site' to 'Customer's Presence' introduces ambiguity, while also changing 'phone company' to 'internet service provider' adds confusion. It is unclear whether 'Customer's Presence' refers to the 'Hosted Site' as previously defined, the language around the internet connection has also been changed. This creates a contradiction with later sections that refer explicitly to the 'Hosted Site', potentially affecting i-on's defined responsibilities to maintain connection. 'internet service provider' is a vague term than 'phone company'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Customer is solely responsible for all content on the Hosted Site, including but not limited to, HTML pages, graphics, sounds, animations, video clips, Java applets, client-site scripts such as JavaScript and VBScript features, ActiveX controls, and other files and/or executable components for use or download by the users of the Hosted Site, as well as the accuracy and validity of any information or data contained within, as well as the overall look-and-feel of the Hosted Site from a user's perspective. The Customer is solely responsible for the ongoing maintenance of such content. The Customer acknowledges that this Agreement is explicitly not an agreement for i-on to provide content creation or maintenance services for the Hosted Site. The Customer is solely responsible for all customer support required by users of Hosted Site. In the case of a problem with the Hosted Site that is the responsibility of i-on according to this Agreement, the Customer shall directly notify i-on, which shall report the resolution of such problem directly to the Customer. If the problem of which i-on is notified is not a problem that is the responsibility of i-on according to this Agreement, the time spent by i-on relating to the incident will count towards the Customer's monthly allocation of Web administration services, and any additional time 3 exceeding such allocation will be billed to the Customer at the rate set forth for such services. At no time will i-on take responsibility for directly interacting with the Customer's users. The Customer acknowledges that this Agreement is explicitly not an agreement for i-on to provide "help desk" services to the users of the Hosted Site. The Customer is solely responsible for all marketing and promotion of the Hosted Site and is solely responsible for generating traffic to the Hosted Site. The Customer is solely responsible for the security of its administrator account(s) and respective password(s) for the Hosted Site, and is solely responsible for any loss of data or damage to the Hosted Site that arises out of any breach of such security. The Customer is solely responsible for any and all advertising on the Hosted Site. The Customer is responsible for any and all software programs, server-side scripts, and/or executable components that are installed on the Hosting Computer for the purpose of providing interactive applications or dynamic content on the Hosted Site. Any such programs, scripts, or components that might affect the stability of the Hosting Computer or interfere with other Web sites on the Hosting Computer must be approved by i-on before being installed on the Hosted Site, i-on reserves the right to deny the Customer permission to install any such programs, scripts, or components, to require additional fees for the installation and/or ongoing operation of any such programs, scripts, or components, or to remove any such programs, scripts, or components, if in i-on's sole discretion they will interfere with the operation of the Hosting Computer or exceed the Customer's monthly allocation of Web administration services.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'Hosted Site' and linked i-on's responsibilities to maintaining connectivity for that specific entity. The changed text replaces 'Hosted Site' with 'Customer's Presence,' introducing ambiguity and a potentially different scope for i-on's responsibilities. This contradicts the 'Contradicted Text,' which repeatedly and specifically defines the Customer's responsibilities regarding the 'Hosted Site.' By changing the term, it creates uncertainty about whether the Customer's responsibilities as outlined in the Contradicted Text still apply as defined or if they are now altered or negated, leading to a direct conflict. The term 'internet service provider' introduces ambiguity compared to 'phone company', further contradicting later sections.

perturbation.original_text: The Customer acknowledges that failure to pay such fees in a timely manner will result in the interruption or discontinuation of services for the Hosted Site.
perturbation.changed_text: The Customer acknowledges that failure to pay such fees in a timely manner will result in the interruption or discontinuation of the deliverables.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'services for the Hosted Site' with 'the deliverables' creates confusion. 'Deliverables' is a vague term that does not relate to any specific part of the services that 'i-on' provides as stated in the text. This could lead to arguments about what exactly will be interrupted if payment is not received on time and therefore cause an In-Text Contradiction, as the original text mentions Hosted Site, but now its 'Deliverables'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: i-on will not be liable under any circumstances for any lost profits or other consequential damages, even if i-on has been advised as to the possibility of such damages. i-on's liability for damages to the Customer for any cause whatsoever, regardless of the form of action, and whether in contract or in tort, including negligence, shall be limited to one (1) month's fees and the remaining portion of any prepaid fees.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'services for the Hosted Site' with 'the deliverables.' This creates an ambiguity regarding what specific services or items will be interrupted due to non-payment. The contradicted text limits i-on's liability to 'one (1) month's fees,' which is directly related to services. If 'deliverables' are interrupted instead of 'services,' it's unclear whether this limitation still applies, potentially leading to a contradiction and affecting the enforceability of the liability clause because it is unclear if the deliverables have fees attached or if the fee applies to 'deliverables' or 'services' creating a strong in-text contradiction.
